intro: The Musk v. Altman trial dominated the week's AI news as both men took the stand, while OpenAI's simultaneous expansion onto AWS signals the company is aggressively moving to reduce its Microsoft dependency ahead of a highly anticipated IPO.

stories.title: Musk v. Altman Trial: Old Friendship, Big Stakes for OpenAI's Future
stories.summary: The long-simmering legal feud between Elon Musk and Sam Altman finally reached a Northern California courtroom this week, and it's genuinely consequential — not just tech-world drama. Musk testified Tuesday that he co-founded OpenAI specifically to prevent what he called a 'Terminator outcome,' framing the lawsuit as existential for humanity, not just business. The core legal question is whether OpenAI is permitted to convert from a nonprofit to a for-profit entity, which is a prerequisite for its anticipated IPO. If the court rules against OpenAI, it could force a dramatic restructuring or even oust current leadership. Musk's testimony largely relitigated the early days of OpenAI's founding, a story he has told before in interviews and to biographer Walter Isaacson, but Tuesday was the first time he said it under oath — which raises the legal stakes significantly. The judge notably warned both Musk and Altman to stop using social media to attack each other outside the courtroom, suggesting both sides have been playing to the public gallery as much as to the bench. Altman published 'Our Principles' on OpenAI's blog the same week, which reads partly like a public-facing counter-narrative. The trial also puts OpenAI's charitable mission — and what it means for nonprofit governance broadly — under scrutiny. Musk's lawyers argue that the original promise to keep AI development in the public interest is being broken by a for-profit pivot. This case will likely define how AI labs can legally structure themselves for years to come, so even if you don't care about the personalities involved, the outcome matters enormously.

stories.title: OpenAI Lands on AWS: Models, Codex, and Managed Agents Now on Amazon Bedrock
stories.summary: The day after OpenAI and Microsoft announced an amended partnership that strips Microsoft's exclusive cloud rights, Amazon moved fast — announcing that OpenAI's GPT models, Codex, and a new Managed Agents service are now available on AWS Bedrock. This is a significant strategic shift. For years, Microsoft Azure was the only cloud where you could run OpenAI's models in an enterprise context; that exclusivity is now gone. The amended Microsoft deal reportedly still gives Microsoft preferential pricing and capacity commitments, but OpenAI is now free to court other hyperscalers, and AWS is clearly eager to be first in line. The new Managed Agents offering is worth watching — it's a hosted service that handles the orchestration layer for agentic workflows, which is increasingly where enterprise AI spend is heading. OpenAI also published an open-source spec called Symphony for Codex orchestration, which turns issue trackers into persistent agent systems. For enterprises already deep in AWS infrastructure, this lowers the friction of adopting OpenAI models considerably. Sam Altman and AWS CEO Matt Garman discussed the deal in an interview, both framing it as complementary rather than competitive with the Microsoft relationship. The broader implication is that OpenAI is behaving more like a model API provider and less like a Microsoft-captive partner, which is exactly what you'd expect from a company eyeing an IPO. Watch for Google Cloud to announce a similar deal shortly.

stories.title: DeepSeek V4 Preview: Longer Context, Open Source, and Still Cheap
stories.summary: Chinese AI lab DeepSeek quietly dropped a preview of V4, its long-awaited new flagship model, and it's worth paying attention to even amid the noise of the Musk trial. The most notable capability improvement is a dramatically extended context window, enabled by a new architectural design that handles large text volumes more efficiently — MIT Tech Review describes this as the model's headline technical achievement. Like all of DeepSeek's previous models, V4 is open source, which means the weights will be publicly available for anyone to fine-tune or deploy. That combination — frontier-competitive capability plus open weights plus Chinese pricing — continues to pressure Western AI labs on cost. DeepSeek's previous models, particularly R1, already caused a minor market panic when they demonstrated near-parity with OpenAI models at a fraction of the training cost. V4 appears to continue that trajectory. The extended context capability is particularly relevant for enterprise use cases like document analysis, codebase reasoning, and long-form research. It also makes DeepSeek more competitive with models like Gemini 3.1, which has had a long-context advantage. The open-source nature of the release means the research community will quickly benchmark it against GPT-5.5 and other recent releases. For developers evaluating model choices, DeepSeek V4 is now a serious contender that deserves testing before defaulting to OpenAI. The geopolitical dimension — a Chinese lab releasing competitive open-source AI — remains a background tension that isn't going away.

stories.title: GitHub Copilot Switches to Usage-Based Pricing as AI Inference Costs Bite
stories.summary: GitHub announced it will start charging Copilot users based on actual AI usage rather than a flat subscription fee, citing 'escalating inference costs' from its heaviest users. This is an honest and somewhat overdue admission that the flat-rate model for AI coding assistants was always going to be economically fragile. A small percentage of power users — think people who have Copilot open all day generating code completions constantly — consume a wildly disproportionate share of inference compute. GitHub has been absorbing that cost, but apparently it's no longer sustainable. The shift to usage-based pricing is the classic move for infrastructure products once the 'land and grab users' phase ends and unit economics start mattering. For most developers who use Copilot occasionally, this probably won't change their bill much. For heavy users running agentic coding sessions or using Copilot for large context refactors, costs could increase meaningfully. The timing is interesting given that OpenAI just released GPT-5.5, which is presumably more expensive to run than previous models, and GitHub will want to pass those costs through. This also signals that AI coding tools are maturing out of the 'subsidized growth' phase — competitors like Cursor, Codeium, and others will face similar pressure. If you're a team lead who approved Copilot subscriptions for your whole org, it's worth auditing actual usage patterns before your next renewal cycle.

stories.title: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Can Autonomously Find and Weaponize Software Vulnerabilities
stories.summary: Anthropic announced Claude Mythos Preview, a model specifically designed to autonomously discover and exploit software vulnerabilities — and the cybersecurity community is paying close attention. This is a significant capability threshold: moving from 'AI that can help a human understand a CVE' to 'AI that can independently hunt for and weaponize new vulnerabilities' is a qualitative leap. IEEE Spectrum's coverage digs into what this means for the future of offensive and defensive security. On the offensive side, this lowers the bar for sophisticated cyberattacks dramatically — a well-resourced attacker (nation-state or otherwise) with access to this capability could automate what currently requires highly skilled human researchers. On the defensive side, the same capability could be used to find and patch vulnerabilities before attackers do, which is Anthropic's stated rationale. The dual-use tension here is real and not easily resolved. Anthropic is reportedly offering Mythos through a controlled red-teaming program rather than open API access, at least initially. The move puts pressure on Google DeepMind and OpenAI, both of whom have been developing similar capabilities. For security teams, this is both a threat model update and a potential tool — but the access controls around who gets to use it matter enormously. This is one of those announcements that deserves more attention than it's getting amid the trial news.

stories.title: Talkie: A 13B Language Model Trained Only on Pre-1930 Text
stories.summary: This is a genuinely fun and scientifically interesting project from a team that includes Alec Radford, one of the original authors of GPT and GPT-2 and co-creator of Whisper. Talkie-1930 is a 13-billion parameter language model trained exclusively on text from before 1930, giving it a 'vintage' linguistic character — it speaks in the idioms, vocabulary, and cadence of the early 20th century. The model is open source and available on Hugging Face at about 53GB. Beyond the novelty factor, this project has genuine research value: it provides a clean test bed for studying how training data distribution shapes model behavior and linguistic register. It's also a fascinating tool for historians, linguists, and writers working in period settings. The involvement of Radford lends it credibility — this isn't just a fun hack, but a thoughtful experiment in understanding what a model 'knows' and 'sounds like' when cut off from decades of contemporary internet text. The Hacker News community scored it highly (661 points), suggesting real developer interest. For practitioners thinking about domain-specific model training, Talkie is a good case study in how deliberately constrained training data produces models with distinctive, useful properties. It's also a reminder that not every interesting AI project needs to be the biggest or most capable — sometimes the most revealing experiments are the constrained ones.

stories.title: AI's Profit Problem: The Missing Step Between Hype and Real Revenue
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review ran a sharp piece on what it calls 'the missing step' between AI hype and actual profit — essentially, that despite enormous investment and capability improvements, most enterprises are still struggling to convert AI deployments into measurable bottom-line impact. The piece uses the South Park 'underpants gnomes' framing (collect underpants → ??? → profit) to describe how many AI strategies currently work in practice. The core issue is that AI tools demonstrably improve individual productivity in demos and pilots, but the gains are hard to capture at scale — partly because workflows aren't redesigned around AI capabilities, and partly because the savings often show up as 'avoided headcount' rather than new revenue, which is politically difficult to count. The data infrastructure problem is a related bottleneck: a companion MIT Tech Review piece notes that most enterprises' data stacks weren't built for AI-scale ingestion and querying, and fixing that is unglamorous, expensive, and slow. This tracks with what's actually happening in the industry — hyperscaler revenue is booming (someone is paying for compute), but enterprise ROI stories remain mostly anecdotal. For engineers being asked to justify AI spending, this framing is useful: the question isn't 'is the model good enough' but 'have we restructured the workflow to actually capture value.' That's a harder organizational problem than a technical one.

stories.title: OpenAI Codex's System Prompt Tells It to Never Talk About Goblins
stories.summary: Someone found a delightful Easter egg buried in OpenAI's open-source Codex repository: the base instructions for the coding agent explicitly include the line 'Never talk about goblins, gremlins, raccoons, trolls, ogres, pigeons, or other animals or creatures unless it is absolutely and unambiguously relevant to the user's query.' The instruction is sitting in a public JSON config file in the Codex-RS models manager. On one level this is funny, and Wired and Simon Willison both covered it with appropriate amusement. But it's also a window into how LLM system prompts actually get written in practice — through accumulated specific prohibitions based on observed model behavior, rather than elegant general rules. Someone, at some point, encountered Codex spontaneously chatting about goblins during a coding session and decided the fix was to add a line to the system prompt. That's very real. It also illustrates the inherent brittleness of prompt-based behavioral control — you can enumerate the creatures you don't want it to discuss, but you can't anticipate everything. For developers building on top of LLM APIs, this is a gentle reminder that system prompts are living documents that evolve in response to weird edge cases, and that's completely normal. The broader implication is that even OpenAI's own production systems use the same messy, iterative prompt engineering that everyone else does.

stories.title: FCC Orders Review of ABC Broadcast Licenses After Jimmy Kimmel Joke Offends Trump
stories.summary: The FCC ordered a license review of ABC's broadcast stations after Jimmy Kimmel made a joke calling Melania Trump an 'expectant widow' — and the speed of the government's response is the story here. The review order came almost immediately after the joke aired, which critics are calling a textbook example of using regulatory machinery to intimidate broadcasters. This matters for the tech and media industry because broadcast licenses are the leverage point through which the government can exercise enormous pressure on content — even if the FCC review ultimately goes nowhere, the chilling effect on editorial decisions is real. ABC is owned by Disney, a major media conglomerate, but smaller broadcasters with less legal firepower would be even more vulnerable to this kind of regulatory pressure. The pattern fits a broader trend of using federal agencies as tools against perceived political opponents. For tech platforms, the parallel concern is regulatory action against social media companies, streaming services, or AI systems that produce politically inconvenient outputs. Ars Technica's headline — 'The clown car is all gassed up' — captures the editorial sentiment, but the underlying machinery being tested is serious. First Amendment lawyers are watching this closely. The outcome of the license review will matter less than whether other broadcasters start self-censoring to avoid being next.

stories.title: Google Expands Pentagon AI Access After Anthropic Refused Domestic Surveillance and Autonomous Weapons Use
stories.summary: Google signed a new contract with the Department of Defense to expand access to its AI systems, stepping into a gap left by Anthropic, which reportedly refused to allow the DoD to use Claude for domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons applications. The contrast between the two companies' stances is striking and worth examining carefully. Anthropic's refusal appears to have been based on its published acceptable use policy, which explicitly prohibits certain military applications — and the company apparently held that line when the DoD pushed. Google's willingness to fill that gap will reignite the internal debates that erupted at Google in 2018 over Project Maven, when thousands of employees signed a letter opposing AI work for drone warfare and several resigned. Google eventually let the Maven contract expire then, but clearly the institutional calculus has shifted. The specific applications Google is enabling aren't fully detailed in the reporting, but 'domestic mass surveillance' and 'autonomous weapons' are the use cases Anthropic declined. For engineers working at AI companies, this is a live ethics question: what applications will your employer accept DoD money to build? For the broader public, the story illustrates that AI safety commitments are tested when large government contracts are on the table. Anthropic's decision to walk away from revenue deserves acknowledgment — it's easy to have principles when nothing is at stake.

stories.title: DOGE Affiliate Now Runs Login.gov — America's Digital Identity Platform
stories.summary: Greg Hogan, described by Wired as a DOGE affiliate, has been installed as the person overseeing Login.gov, the US government's shared identity platform used by millions of Americans to access federal services. This is happening at the same time the government is pushing to integrate driver's license and passport data into Login.gov, effectively building what one insider quoted by Wired calls 'a national ID.' The combination of a national identity database and DOGE-affiliated leadership is raising serious alarms among privacy and civil liberties advocates. Login.gov is the single sign-on system for a wide range of federal agencies — if you've used it to access benefits, tax records, or other government services, your data is in there. DOGE's track record on data handling and security practices has been controversial, with multiple reports of inadequate security protocols during its broader government IT access push. The move to expand Login.gov to include physical identity documents (driver's licenses, passports) would make it significantly more sensitive than it currently is. For technologists, this raises concrete questions about data architecture, access controls, and who has administrative rights to the system. For citizens, it's a reminder that government digital infrastructure decisions have direct privacy implications. The 'national ID' framing is politically loaded but technically accurate — a unified federal identity system with biometric document integration is, functionally, a national ID.

stories.title: Supreme Court Appears Split on Geofence Warrants — A Case That Could Reshape Digital Privacy
stories.summary: The US Supreme Court heard arguments in Chatrie v. United States, a case about whether police can use 'geofence warrants' — court orders demanding that tech companies like Google hand over data on every device present in a geographic area during a specific time window — to identify criminal suspects. The justices appeared genuinely divided, which means this case could go either way. Geofence warrants are controversial because they're essentially dragnet searches: instead of identifying a suspect first and then seeking their data, police cast a wide net and sift through innocent bystanders' location history to find someone who might be guilty. Google's Sensorvault database, which stores historical location data from Android devices, has been the primary target of these warrants. The constitutional question is whether this kind of bulk location data collection constitutes an unreasonable search under the Fourth Amendment, particularly in light of the Supreme Court's 2018 Carpenter decision, which required warrants for cell-site location records. A ruling allowing geofence warrants would give law enforcement a powerful surveillance tool with minimal judicial oversight. A ruling against them would force police to develop more targeted investigative methods. For tech companies, the outcome determines whether their location databases can be compelled as investigative fishing nets. This is one of the most consequential digital privacy cases the Court has taken in years.

stories.title: Australia Passes Law Forcing Big Tech to Pay for News or Face a 2.25% Revenue Tax
stories.summary: Australia enacted a law requiring large tech platforms — think Google and Meta — to either negotiate payment deals with news publishers or face a 2.25% revenue tax, with the rate dropping to 1.5% if enough deals are struck. The law is projected to generate between A$200 million and A$250 million for Australian journalism annually. This is an evolution of Australia's earlier News Media Bargaining Code, which passed in 2021 and prompted Meta to briefly block Australian news before cutting deals. The new law creates a more direct financial incentive structure: platforms are better off making deals with publishers than paying the tax, so the government expects most compliance to happen through negotiated agreements rather than taxation. This model is being watched closely by the EU, UK, and Canada, all of whom have been wrestling with similar legislation. For tech platforms, this represents an ongoing shift from 'we aggregate your content for free and you benefit from the traffic' to 'you pay for the content that makes our platforms valuable.' The broader question is whether mandatory payments actually preserve journalism or just prop up incumbent publishers while starving newer digital outlets. The Australian experiment will be a real-world test of that question over the next few years.

stories.title: Ghostty Terminal Leaves GitHub — And the Broader Implications
stories.summary: Ghostty, the fast and modern terminal emulator that became a developer darling, announced it is leaving GitHub and moving its repository to a self-hosted alternative. The project's creator Mitchell Hashimoto (yes, the HashiCorp founder) cited concerns about platform dependency, Microsoft ownership of GitHub, and the desire to control the project's own infrastructure. The Hacker News community responded strongly, with 2,175 points making it one of the top stories of the week — which suggests the sentiment resonates broadly among developers. This follows a pattern of open-source projects quietly reconsidering their GitHub dependency, partly due to Microsoft's ownership and partly due to GitHub's Copilot training on public code, which remains a contentious issue. The practical implications for contributors are minor — git is git, and most developers can adapt to a different host — but the symbolic weight is significant. Ghostty is a high-profile, well-respected project, and its departure signals that GitHub's dominance of open-source hosting is not unconditional. For developers maintaining open-source projects, this is worth following: the post-GitHub landscape is slowly taking shape, with Forgejo, Codeberg, and self-hosted Gitea instances all gaining momentum. The timing, amid GitHub's pivot to monetizing AI through Copilot, seems unlikely to be coincidental.

stories.title: GitHub RCE Vulnerability CVE-2026-3854: What You Need to Know
stories.summary: A remote code execution vulnerability in GitHub (CVE-2026-3854) was disclosed and analyzed by Wiz Research, scoring 304 points on Hacker News. RCE vulnerabilities in GitHub are particularly serious because of the platform's central role in software supply chains — a successful exploit could potentially allow an attacker to inject malicious code into repositories, tamper with CI/CD pipelines, or gain access to private code and secrets. Wiz's breakdown covers the attack vector and proof-of-concept details. GitHub published an availability update acknowledging the incident. The specifics of exploitability and whether it was actively exploited before patching are critical details to check in the full disclosure. For teams running GitHub Enterprise on-premises, patching timelines are more urgent since GitHub.com is presumably already patched by GitHub. This is a good reminder to audit what's in your GitHub Actions workflows, which have become a significant attack surface in their own right — a compromised runner or poisoned action can have supply chain consequences far beyond the immediate repository. If your organization uses GitHub for anything security-sensitive, reviewing the CVE details and GitHub's incident report should be on your to-do list today.

stories.title: Warp Terminal Goes Open Source
stories.summary: Warp, the AI-powered terminal that raised significant VC funding and built a loyal developer following with its modern UI and built-in AI features, announced it is going open source. This is a notable strategic pivot for a company that has been building a proprietary product. The move likely reflects both competitive pressure from open-source alternatives and a recognition that developer tool adoption is significantly easier when the code is open. Warp scored 219 points on Hacker News, suggesting genuine community interest. The open-source release means developers can now inspect the codebase, contribute, and self-host — addressing a common complaint about vendor lock-in for a tool that sits at the center of a developer's daily workflow. The business model question is the interesting one: Warp will presumably continue offering a paid cloud tier or enterprise features, similar to how GitLab and HashiCorp operated before their various pivots. For developers who had been avoiding Warp precisely because it was closed-source and required a login, this removes the primary objection. Combined with Ghostty leaving GitHub, this week has been an interesting one for the terminal emulator space specifically — a corner of developer tooling that usually moves slowly.

stories.title: China Blocks Meta's $2B Acquisition of AI Startup Manus
stories.summary: Chinese regulators have blocked Meta's $2 billion acquisition of Manus, a Chinese AI startup that gained significant attention earlier this year for its autonomous agent capabilities. The deal, which had been under scrutiny for months, represents a significant and somewhat surprising regulatory intervention — China blocking a foreign acquisition of a domestic AI company is a reversal of the usual direction of cross-border tech deal friction. Manus attracted global attention when it went viral for its ability to autonomously complete complex multi-step tasks, and the acquisition would have given Meta access to both the talent and the underlying technology. The blocking of the deal fits into China's broader pattern of tightening control over domestic AI assets deemed strategically important. For Meta, which has been investing heavily in AI across multiple fronts, this is a notable setback — Manus was reportedly one of the more technically impressive agent systems available, and integrating it into Meta's AI stack would have been meaningful. The decision also signals that the geopolitics of AI acquisitions are becoming as fraught as semiconductor trade restrictions. For anyone tracking the global AI landscape, this is a reminder that some of the most interesting AI development is happening in China, and Western companies' ability to access it through M&A is increasingly limited.

stories.title: Meta's AI Training Contractor Faces Layoffs of 700+ Workers in Ireland
stories.summary: More than 700 workers employed by Covalen, a Meta contractor in Ireland responsible for training and evaluating Meta's AI systems, are at risk of layoff, according to documents obtained by Wired. These are the data annotation and RLHF workers who do the grinding, repetitive work of making large language models actually useful — rating outputs, flagging problems, providing feedback that shapes model behavior. The quote from a worker — 'It's undignified' — captures the human cost of a pattern that's becoming common across the AI industry: companies use contract labor for the least glamorous but most essential AI work, then automate or offshore it once the model is good enough. Ireland has become a significant hub for this kind of AI contractor work partly due to its EU membership and English language, and the scale of potential layoffs is notable. The irony is that these workers are, in a meaningful sense, the people who taught the models that are now being used to replace them. For engineers building AI systems, it's worth sitting with that for a moment. The broader industry pattern — using human labor intensively to bootstrap AI capabilities, then reducing that labor as AI improves — is going to be a recurring story across many sectors.

stories.title: Humanoid Robots Start Sorting Luggage at Tokyo's Haneda Airport
stories.summary: Japan Airlines is running a real-world pilot of humanoid robots handling luggage sorting and potentially cabin cleaning at Haneda Airport, addressing a genuine and worsening labor shortage problem in Japan. This isn't a demo reel — it's an operational test with actual traveler luggage going through the system. Japan has been one of the most aggressive early adopters of automation for demographic reasons: the country has an aging population, a shrinking workforce, and cultural resistance to large-scale immigration, making robotics a genuine economic necessity rather than just a curiosity. Baggage handling is a good early use case for humanoid robots because the environment is semi-structured (airport infrastructure doesn't change much), the tasks are physically demanding and injury-prone for humans, and the cost of errors — delayed bags — is annoying but not catastrophic. The bidirectional robotaxi design story from Zoox (also in today's news) and this luggage robot story both point to the same trend: purpose-built autonomous systems are making the most progress in constrained, well-defined physical environments. The broader question for humanoid robotics is whether the 'general purpose' framing is actually the right one, or whether task-specific systems in specific verticals will commercialize first. Japan's labor constraints mean it will be a leading indicator for where humanoid robotics actually becomes economically viable.
